coredata.dc:title: A glassy carbon electrode modified with carbon quantum dots and polyalizarin yellow R dyes for enhanced electrocatalytic oxidation and nanomolar detection of l-cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Amini, Nader
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Gholivand, Mohammad Bagher
Barati, Ali
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemical sensor for the detection of l-cysteine was proposed based on immobilizing poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon quantum dots film on glassy carbon electrode. Hydrothermal treatment was used to prepare carbon quantum dots. Transmission electron microscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were used for characterization of carbon quantum dots. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry and amperommetry were utilized to confirm the successful stepwise assembly procedure of the sensor. The electrocatalytic behaviors of the sensor were also investigated by cyclic voltammetry and amperommetry. Results showed that poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots exhibited a remarkable electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of l-cysteine under optimal conditions. The electrocatalytic response of the sensor was proportional to the l-cysteine concentration in the range of (0.3 to 3.6μM) and (3.9 to 7.2μM) with a limit of detection and sensitivity of 90nM and 0.482μA/μM, respectively. The modified electrode demonstrated many advantages such as simple preparation, high sensitivity, low detection of limit, excellent catalytic activity at physiological pH values, short response time, and remarkable antifouling property toward l-cysteine and its oxidation product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: l-Cysteine
Carbon dots
Alizarin yellow R
FTIR
Amperometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and synthesis of carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical characterization of poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
Electrocatalytic oxidation of cysteine on poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
Amperometric detection of cysteine at poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
Comparison of the figures of merit of the proposed sensor with those of previous electrochemical methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The continuous CVs for the electropolymerization of alizarin yellow R on carbon dots GCE in 0.1M phosphate buffer (pH11) at potential scan rate 100mVs−1. (B) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of (a) bare GC electrode (b) carbon QDs/GCE (c) carbon QDs/alizarin yellow/GCE in 5mM probe Fe (CN)6 4−/3−.

Cyclic voltammetry response of unmodified electrode in the absence of cysteine (a) unmodified (b) carbon QDs modified (c) alizarin yellow modified (d) and (e) carbon QDs/alizarin yellow modified GC electrode in the presence 50μM of cysteine, scan rate 20mVs−1, pH7.

Cyclic voltammetry response of a GCE modified with carbon QDs/alizarin in a phosphate buffer (pH7) containing 50μM of cysteine at different scan rates (from inner to outer) 10–100mVs−1. Inset, plot of peak current vs, v1/2.
Amperommetric responses at rotating modified GC electrode (rotating speed 2000rpm) held at 0.55V in buffer solution (pH7), inset, A shows plot of the peak currents as a function of concentration.
Analytical parameters of several modified electrodes for determination of cysteine.

row.entry: Cobalt(II) saqlophen in to carbon past
Self-assembleed of cobalt and iron phthalocyanine on gold
Lead phthalocyanine incorporated into poly(vinylchloride) on pyrolytic graphite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of cysteine in a syrup sample.

head.ce:title: A glassy carbon electrode modified with carbon quantum dots and polyalizarin yellow R dyes for enhanced electrocatalytic oxidation and nanomolar detection of l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kurdistan Environmental Health Research Center, Kurdistan University of Medical Sciences, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel electrochemical sensor for the detection of l-cysteine was proposed based on immobilizing poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon quantum dots film on glassy carbon electrode. Hydrothermal treatment was used to prepare carbon quantum dots. Transmission electron microscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy were used for characterization of carbon quantum dots. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry and amperommetry were utilized to confirm the successful stepwise assembly procedure of the sensor. The electrocatalytic behaviors of the sensor were also investigated by cyclic voltammetry and amperommetry. Results showed that poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots exhibited a remarkable electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of l-cysteine under optimal conditions. The electrocatalytic response of the sensor was proportional to the l-cysteine concentration in the range of (0.3 to 3.6μM) and (3.9 to 7.2μM) with a limit of detection and sensitivity of 90nM and 0.482μA/μM, respectively. The modified electrode demonstrated many advantages such as simple preparation, high sensitivity, low detection of limit, excellent catalytic activity at physiological pH values, short response time, and remarkable antifouling property toward l-cysteine and its oxidation product.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine is a typical thiol compound and classified as a hydrophilic non-essential amino acid even if essential for humans. Because of high reactivity of the thiol group, cysteine is an important structural and functional component of many proteins and enzymes [1–2]. Moreover, cysteine or compounds containing cysteine derivatives are widely used in many pharmaceutical, cosmetic and food products [3]. Thus, determination protocols of cysteine compounds have been developed for both clinical and industrial purposes. Among existing methods for cysteine detection, chromatographic (especially HPLC) and spectroscopic methods are the most abundant assays. However, chromatographic separation based methods present some disadvantages as the derivatization and extraction stage of the procedure can increase analysis time and the total cost of the assay. In addition, due to a lack of strong chromophore in cysteine structure, its determination by absorbance measurements is very difficult. Therefore, spectrophotometric detection is based on derivatization with cromogenic reagents [4]. However, a fast protocol with minimal sample pretreatment is needed for the determination of thiols. Electrochemical techniques can be nominated as the most favored methods for the determination of thiols. The electrochemical analysis of thiols at the surface of conventional electrodes (e.g., glassy carbon and gold electrodes) encounters such drawbacks as a sluggish electrochemical process and requirement of high overpotentials [5–7]. To overcome these drawbacks many strategies have been employed. One of these strategies is modification of the surface of the conventional electrodes with different materials such as a polymeric layer [8], composite materials [9], metallic complexes [10], nanoparticles [11] and organic compounds [12]. For examples, polypyrrole and graphene quantum dots @ Prussian Blue hybrid film [13], nickel oxide nanoparticles on N-doped reduced graphene oxide [14], polyoxometalates and Au@2Ag core–shell nanoparticles [15] have been utilized for determination of l-cysteine. l-Cysteine (l-CySH) as a thiol, due to its crucial role in biological systems, has been the subject of many electrochemical studies investigating both mechanism and detection aspects [16].

Quantum dots (QDs), also called semiconductor nanocrystals, are a class of nanoparticles containing group II–VI elements or group III–V elements, with diameter between 1 and 10nm. Due to their size-tunable, chemically functionalizable surface, electronic property and catalytic effect, they are widely used as modified material in electrochemical sensors [17–18]. Among quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) is the most promising. Carbon dots (CDs) constitute an important class of carbon nanostructures owing to their good photoluminescence (PL) properties, high stability without photobleaching and obviously low toxicity in comparison to other quantum dots. CDs possess a large surface area and efficient catalytic activity suggesting that it may be an ideal electrode material with promising applications in electrochemistry.

Carbon dots have been used in electrochemical sensors to accelerate the electron transfer process between sensing interface and the electrode and promote the redox reaction processes. For example, reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite can be used as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine [19]. N-doped carbon dots exhibited a highly sensitive electrochemical response toward dopamine [20]. Carbon dots/MWCNT/Nafion/MWCNT modified electrode can be used for simultaneous determination of resorcinol, catechol and hydroquinone [21]. The nanocomposite of carbon dots, chitosan and hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of 2,4-DC dichlorophenol [22]. Moreover, Li et al. showed that the CDs/octahedral Cu2O nanocomposites exhibited excellent electrocatalytic performance to glucose oxidation and H2O2 reduction [23]. Gao et al. reported that polyamidoamine dendrimers capped-carbon dots nanocomposites with excellent conductivity, stability and biocompatibility on the surface of electrode can be designed as an immobilized matrix for sensitive immunosensing of alpha-fetoprotein [24].

Compared with other electrode modified materials, the electrochemical research of CDs will gain more and more importance due to the low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, simple synthesis, economy and remarkable conductivity. However, due to insolubility of CDs in most solvents the preparation of a stable CDs film on the surface of the electrode is difficult. One of the many efforts that have been made to overcome this drawback is based on electropolymerization in the presence of dyes [25]. The literature survey has revealed the existence of a report about the usage of CDs film as modifier on the surface of the electrode [26].

Conducting polymers have been introduced as ideal support material for an electrochemical sensor [20]. Good stability, reproducibility, and homogeneity in electrochemical deposition and strong adherence to electrode surface have been reported as their advantages. Organic dyes such as neutral red [27–29], toluidine blue [30], alizarin red S [31], congo red [32–33], nile blue A [34], thionine [35], pyrocatechol violet [36] and alizarin yellow R [37] as particular conducting polymer monomer have been successfully used to prepare electrochemical sensors. It is certain that the composite film produced by combining organic dyes with nanophase carbonic materials as modifier will become the hotspot of chemical modified electrodes field. For example, Hu et al. [38] presented a nitrite sensor using CNTs/poly (alizarine red S) modified electrode; Wan et al. [39] reported a kojic acid sensor based on an alizarine MWCNTs modified electrode.

Alizarin yellow R (AYR), an azo dye with a salicylic acid structure, is occasionally used as a pH indicator [40] and a metallochromic indicator [41]. This organic dye has been used for electrode surface designation due to its good stability and electrochemical activity. This paper is believed to be the first to describe the deposition of carbon dots onto a surface by in-situ electropolymerization in the presence of AYR.

The main objective of this work is to introduce the potential application of CDs/AYR film-modified electrode in the determination of l-cysteine. The resulting CDs/AYR composite film electrode exhibited good electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of l-cysteine under optimal conditions. The amperometric responses had a linear relationship with the l-cysteine concentrations in the range of 0.3 to 3.6μM and 3.9 to 7.2μM. The sensitivity of the sensor is 0.482μA/μM, and the detection limit was low to 90nM. This sensor featured low detection limit, good sensitivity, short response time, remarkable antifouling property and excellent catalytic activity at physiological pH values for l-cysteine determination. Moreover, the sensor has been successfully applied to detect l-cysteine in drug samples.

Alizarin yellow R, l-cysteine and glucose were purchased from Aldrich and used as received. Buffer solutions (0.1M) were prepared from di-sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), hydrogen chloride (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH), all obtained from Merck. All electrochemical experiments were carried out at 25±0.10°C.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a computer controlled μ-Autolab modular electrochemical system (Eco Chemie U/techt, The Netherlands) driven with GPES software (Eco Chemie). A conventional three-electrode system consisting of an Ag/AgCl [KCl (sat)] reference electrode, Pt wire auxiliary electrode and (unmodified or modified) glassy carbon working electrodes was employed. A personal computer was used for data storage and processing. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained with a Zeiss EM10C microscope. The FTIR spectra were recorded by Perkin-Elmer Spectrum BX FT-IR spectrometer. The pH values of the solutions were determined with Metrohm pH-meter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and synthesis of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The details of synthesis and characterization of carbon quantum dots are given in Supporting Information (SI). Briefly, hydrothermal treatment was used to prepare CDs according to previously published method [42]. The synthesized CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and FTIR spectroscopy and the results are shown in Figs. S1A and B respectively.

Prior to modification, the bare GCE was mechanically polished with alumina paste of different grades to mirror finish, rinsed and ultrasonicated (3min) in ethanol and distilled water to remove the adsorbed particles. Then 2mL carbon dot solution was added to 5mL ethanol, and then ultrasonicated for 30min to form a homogeneous carbon dot (CD) solution. Then 5μL of the above solution was dripped onto cleaned GC electrode surface and then dried in the air at room temperature. After drying, the CDs/GC electrode was obtained. The electropolymerization of alizarin yellow R on the CDs/GCE was carried out by circulary sweeping CDs/GCE from −1.0V to 1.2V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1 in 0.1M phosphate buffer (pH11) for 15cycles. The obtained modified electrode was dipped into double-distilled water for a few minutes to remove the adsorbed dyes. For comparison, PAYR/GCE was also fabricated by the same aforementioned procedure.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were carried out in the presence of a 5.0mMK3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) mixture as a redox probe in 0.1M PBS (pH7.0) at a bias potential of 0.28V and in the frequency range of 0.01–104 Hz. The morphology of samples was characterized with transmission electron microscopy. Cyclic voltammetric and amperometric techniques were used for l-cysteine detection. The cyclic voltammogram (CV) of 10mL phosphate buffer solution with pH7 was used for background correction. Then l-cysteine solution with different concentrations was added and their CVs were used for calibration curve construction. The CVs were recorded in the range of 0–0.9V. The amperometric response of l-cysteine on PAYR/CDs/GCE was obtained by addition of successive aliquots of l-cysteine in phosphate buffer solution (pH7) under the constant potential of 0.55V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A shows the cyclic voltammograms of electropolymerization of AYR on the CDs/GCE. The electropolymerization was associated with the appearance of two anodic peaks at about −0.3V and +0.6V, and a cathodic peak at −0.75V; all peaks' currents increased with further scanning, reflecting the continuous growth of the conductive poly (alizarin yellow R). The poly (alizarin yellow R) was successfully deposited on the surface of the electrode up to 15cycles and further scanning (>15cycles) had no significant change in the peaks' currents. Therefore, the 15cycles were chosen to fabricate the poly (alizarin yellow R) carbon dots glassy carbon electrode (PAYR/CDs/GCE).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective and convenient tool to monitor electrochemical behavior of the stepwise fabrication process of the modified electrode. Fig. 1B shows the impedance spectra represented as Nyquist plots (Zim vs. Zre) for bare GCE, CDs/GCE and PAYR/CDs/GCE in 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] as an electrochemical probe. In EIS, the semicircle diameter equals electron-transfer resistance (Rct) which controls the electron-transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode interface. Curve a represents the EIS plot of the bare GCE. After assembly of the CDs on the electrode surface, the EIS of the CDs/GC electrode generated more interfacial resistance. The same behavior has been reported by assembly of other quantum dots [43]. When PAYR as a conductive polymer was assembled onto the CDs/GC electrode, a decrease in the interfacial resistance was observed (curve c). This proves that poly alizarine present in poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots film enhance electron shuttling between reactant and the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic oxidation of cysteine on poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used for the study of electro-oxidation of cysteine at the surface of different electrodes. Fig. 2 shows the CV of modified and unmodified electrodes in the absence and presence of 50μM cysteine in a 0.1M buffer solution of pH7. At the bare GCE no observable oxidation peak in the absence (curve a) and the presence (curve b) of cysteine was detected which shows that the electro-oxidation of cysteine requires a large overpotential. When CDs were immobilized onto GC electrode, (CDs/GCE) an oxidation peak for the analyte was observed; its peak current increased and peak potential shifted toward negative values (curve c) which may be due to electrocatalytic activity of carbon dots. The same behavior was perceived when the GCE was modified by alizarin polymer free CDs (curve d), but the anodic peak of cysteine appeared at a more negative potential relative to that of CDs/GCE without considerable enhancement in peak current. Modification of CDs/GCE with alizarin polymer (curve e) not only decreased the oxidation peak potential but increased the peak current relative to that of CDs/GCE which might be due to the synergic effect of both mediators employed in the PAYR/CDs/GCE.

In order to optimize the catalytic response of modified electrode toward cysteine oxidation, the influence of pH solution on response of the PAYR/CDs/GCE to cysteine oxidation was investigated. Therefore, the CVs of the phosphate buffer solutions containing 100μM cysteine at pH ranges 2 to 10 were recorded. The results illustrated that the maximum response of the modified electrode (PAYR/CDs/GCE) appeared at physiological pH values (6–8), therefore pH=7 was selected as the optimal pH for subsequent uses.

The effect of the potential scan rate on the response of the PAYR/CDs/GCE immersed in phosphate buffer solution containing 50μM cysteine (pH=7) was also investigated. The plot of anodic peak current versus square root of scan rate is linear indicating that the process is controlled by diffusion as expected for a catalytic system (Fig. 3 ). Moreover, the peak potential of the wave is dependent on the scan rate which confirmed the irreversibility of the process.

The performance of the proposed modified electrode (PAYR/CDs/GCE) in cysteine solutions with different concentrations was evaluated (Fig. S2 in supplemental information). It is very clear that the modified electrode has a rapid response and high sensitivity toward cysteine. The oxidation current of cysteine is proportional to its concentration over the range from 1μM to 200μM, obeying the following equation by cyclic voltammetry method: I(μA)=0.0126 [cysteine]μM+0.3269 with R2 =0.9903.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric detection of cysteine at poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to estimate the low detection limit, a more sensitive method toward cyclic voltammetry is prefered. Therefore, amperometry under stirred condition was employed. Fig. 4A displays the typical hydrodynamic amperograms of the rotating PAYR/CDs/GC electrode (2000rpm) with successive injection of cysteine at an applied potential of 0.55V vs. reference electrode. As shown, a well defined response was observed during the successive addition of 0.3μM of cysteine. These results demonstrate a stable and efficient catalytic property of the modified electrode. There are two linear relationships between response currents and cysteine concentrations in ranges of 0.3–3.9μM and 3.9–7.2μM (Fig. 4B), The linear least square equation for the first linear range of 0.3–3.6μM is I(nA)=0.482 [cysteine]μM−0.0656 with a correlation coefficient of 0.995, indicating that the regression line is very well suited to the experimental data. Therefore, the regression equation can be used for determination of unknown samples. The detection limit (signal to noise of 3) and sensitivity were 90nM and 0.482μA/μM, respectively.

After the optimization and establishment of the determined method for the preparation of the PAYR/CDs/GC sensor, various amino acids such as glycine, phenylalanine, histidine, cystine and tryptophan were examined with respect to their interference with the determination of cysteine. According to the experimental result, glycine, cystine, phenylalanine and histidine did not interfere in determining cysteine with 80, 50, 10 and 5 fold excess over cysteine concentration. The interference for tryptophan appeared in equimolar concentration in their solution with cysteine. (Figs. S3A and B were shown for histidine and tryptophan respectively in Supplementary information).

Cystein concentration level of 100μM was selected to examine the repeatability of the voltammetric measurements of the modified electrode (PAYR/CDs/GCE) in phosphate buffer (pH7.0). The relative standard deviation, RSD, was found to be 4% for five successive assays. The long-time storage stability of the sensor was examined over a 30-day period. When the sensor was stored at room temperature and measured intermittently (every 2days), after 30days of storage the sensor retained about 82% of its original response. These results indicate good repeatability and stability for the proposed modified electrode for cysteine determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the figures of merit of the proposed sensor with those of previous electrochemical methods
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was compared with several other methods that have been developed for cysteine determination. The results are summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, the linear dynamic range and lower detection limit of this work are better or comparable with those of other techniques that are described in Table 1 [44–52].

In order to demonstrate its practical application, this sensor was used to determine cysteine in cysteine syrup using the recommended procedure. The standard addition method was used for the analysis of the real sample. The data given in Table 2 shows satisfactory results. Recoveries of 98–108% of l-cysteine from syrup sample (n=3) were obtained using the modified electrode.

In the present work, we introduced a new modified electrode based on immobilizing of poly (alizarin yellow R)/carbon dots on the glassy carbon electrode. The proposed electrode can be used for detection of low level concentration of cysteine at physiological pH value. Electrooxidation of cysteine at the surface of modified electrode was studied. The PAYR/CDs/GCE showed very efficient catalytic activity in the electrochemical oxidation of cysteine. Finally, this method was also examined as a sensitive, simple, and precise electrochemical sensor for the determination of cysteine in real sample.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2016.11.004.
